1: The Transjordan settlement request opens the final section of Numbers. The tribes of Reuben and Gad observe that the already-conquered territories east of the Jordan — Jazer and Gilead — are prime grazing land (meqom miqneh, livestock country). The fact that they have 'very large herds' (miqneh rav me'od) motivates the request: pastoral wealth drives this claim. The echo of Lot's choice in Genesis 13:10-11 is unmistakable — Lot 'lifted his eyes' and saw the well-watered Jordan valley; the Reubenites and Gadites 'saw the land of Jazer and Gilead.' Both prioritize visible abundance over covenant commitments.
2: They bring the request to the full leadership triad: Moses, Eleazar the priest, and the community leaders (nesiei ha'edah). The formal submission to the three-tiered authority structure reflects the gravity of the request — this is not a private negotiation but a public covenant claim. Eleazar's inclusion alongside Moses is significant: as Aaron's successor, his presence represents the priestly establishment that will survive the wilderness generation.
3: The cities they enumerate — Ataroth, Dibon, Jazer, Nimrah, Heshbon, Elealeh, Sebam, Nebo, Beon — are all in the Transjordan, east of the Dead Sea and northern Jordan. These are territories already conquered from Sihon king of the Amorites and Og king of Bashan (Numbers 21:21-35). The petitioners are not asking for unconquered territory; they are asking for land that YHWH has already given into Israel's hand — land that already flows with the provision of good pasture.
4: ha'aretz asher hikah YHWH lifney adat Yisrael eretz miqneh hi: 'The land which YHWH struck down before the congregation of Israel is livestock country.' The petitioners ground their request in YHWH's own action: the land is available because YHWH gave it. This is the strongest possible argument — they are not asking for what has not been conquered but for what YHWH has already given. The theological problem is not the land's availability but what the request will signal to the rest of the congregation about commitment to the Canaan campaign.
5: al ta'avireinu et haYarden: 'Do not make us cross the Jordan.' The phrase is the crux of Moses's alarm: the Gadites and Reubenites are asking to opt out of the Jordan-crossing, which is the central act of inheriting the promised land. The Jordan crossing is the covenant's geographic fulfillment; to not cross is to position oneself outside the central promise. Moses immediately connects this to the spies' failure — the language of 'not crossing' activates the Num 13-14 memory.
6: ha'acheichem yavo'u lamilchamah ve'atem teshvu po: 'Are your brothers to go off to war while you settle here?' Moses's first response is a question that exposes the moral problem: their comfort while the rest of Israel fights creates the same division of sacrifice that tribal solidarity is meant to prevent. The question mirrors the challenge to shirkers in any shared military obligation — but here the shared obligation is the covenant-gift of the promised land itself.
7: lamah teniche'un et lev beney Yisrael: 'Why would you discourage (niche') the Israelites?' The verb nache' (Hiphil of nua' = to make waver/discourage) is the same word used in 32:9 for what the spies did to the congregation. Moses hears the Gadite-Reubenite request as a structural repetition of the spy-disaster: just as the spies' negative report turned the congregation's heart away from entering the land, so the visible decision of two large tribes to settle outside Canaan will discourage the remaining tribes from pressing into the promise.
8: Moses appeals to historical precedent: 'Your ancestors did the same thing when I sent them from Kadesh-barnea.' The spy narrative (Num 13-14) is the primary theological frame Moses applies to the current situation. The parallel is exact: the spies were sent to scout a land YHWH had promised to give; the spies returned and discouraged the congregation; YHWH's anger blazed; forty years of wandering followed. Moses sees the same pattern repeating with the next generation.
9: The Valley of Eshcol was where the spies cut the enormous cluster of grapes — the tangible proof that Canaan was the promised land. The spies 'surveyed the land' and 'turned the heart of the Israelites from going into the land YHWH had given them.' The phrase vayenifu et lev beney Yisrael (they caused the heart of Israel to waver) uses the same nache' vocabulary as v7: the ancestors' sin was precisely the sin Moses fears the Gadites and Reubenites are about to repeat.
10: vayichar af YHWH bayom hahu vayishava: 'The LORD's anger blazed that day and he swore.' YHWH's oath-response to the spy generation's failure (Numbers 14:20-35) was the most severe judgment in the wilderness narrative: the entire generation over 20 must die in the desert before the next generation enters. Moses invokes that oath precisely here to make the Gadites and Reubenites aware of the category of sin they are approaching.
11: The oath's content: 'Not one of the men who came out of Egypt, twenty years old or above, will ever see the land I swore to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.' The Abrahamic covenant — the three patriarchal names — frames the promised land as the goal of a covenant stretching back four generations. The land is not a real-estate transaction but a covenant inheritance. Failing to pursue it is covenant disloyalty of the highest order.
12: ki male'u acharey YHWH: 'they followed YHWH fully.' The phrase applied to Caleb and Joshua — mille'u acharey YHWH (lit. they filled/were full behind YHWH) — is the theological standard against which the spy generation failed and against which Moses will measure the Gadites and Reubenites. 'Full-following' is the antithesis of the wavering heart. The conditional promise Moses offers in v20-22 is built on this same standard: 'if you truly arm yourselves before YHWH' — the Gadites and Reubenites can demonstrate full-following by keeping their commitment.
13: The forty-year wandering is presented explicitly as divine punishment: 'The LORD's anger burned against Israel and he made them wander in the wilderness forty years, until the whole generation who had done evil in YHWH's sight had finished.' The word tamam (to come to an end/finish) describes the death of the guilty generation — the forty years are not arbitrary but the time required for the disobedient generation to die out. Every year of wandering represents the covenant's demand that covenant-refusal has consequences.
14: vataqumun tachteyhem torevet anashim chata'im: 'And here you are — a brood of sinful men — risen in your fathers' footsteps.' The phrase tarbut anashim chata'im (increase/brood of sinful men) is Moses's harshest language in the chapter — he calls the petitioners the direct offspring of the guilty generation, implying that the same sinful pattern is being replicated generationally. 'To stoke the LORD's fierce anger against Israel' — Moses sees the request as triggering the same divine wrath that burned against the fathers.
15: ki tishuvu me'acharav: 'If you turn away from following him.' The conditional threat: if Reuben and Gad stay behind, their defection will trigger a new wilderness abandonment for all Israel. 'He will again abandon them in the wilderness' — the word od (again) invokes the first abandonment as the pattern to be repeated. Moses's response is not merely personal disappointment but prophetic warning: the tribes' choice affects the entire covenant community. Individual covenant decisions ripple out to the whole people.
16: The tribes respond with a concrete counter-proposal: 'We will build sheepfolds here for our livestock and towns for our little ones.' They do not dispute Moses's theological framework — they accept the obligation to support the Canaan campaign; they propose a way to do so while also securing their families and flocks. The proposal is practical and essentially fair: leave dependents and property settled in the east; go west as armed vanguard. The construction of cities for children (taph) and sheepfolds (gidroth tzon) is the domestic security that makes the military commitment possible.
17: va'anachnu ne'chalets cheushim lifney beney Yisrael: 'We ourselves will arm for battle and march ahead of the Israelites.' Ne'chaletz (armed/girded for battle) is the military readiness term — not merely alongside Israel but ahead of them, as the vanguard. The proposal is actually more than Moses asked for: they offer not to parallel Israel's advance but to lead it. The phrase 'until every tribe is settled in its place' defines the duration of their military obligation: until all of Israel has received its inheritance, not merely until their own territory is secure.
18: lo nashuv el batteinu ad hithnachel beney Yisrael: 'We will not return home until every Israelite has received his inheritance.' The completeness of the commitment is explicit — not 'until we've done our share' but until every Israelite is settled. The word ish nachalatho (each man his inheritance) uses the covenant-inheritance vocabulary that frames the entire land distribution. The Gadites and Reubenites are not exempting themselves from covenant solidarity but expressing it in a concrete form.
19: The tribes clarify why they are not taking an inheritance in Canaan proper: 'because our inheritance has come to us on this side of the Jordan, on the east.' The Transjordan territory is their portion — they accept the covenant-promise in its eastern form. This verse resolves the ambiguity of their request: they are not refusing the covenant-inheritance but locating it in the already-conquered Transjordan rather than in the not-yet-conquered Cisjordan. From their perspective, YHWH has already given them their inheritance.
20: im ta'asun zo't: 'If you will truly do this.' Moses's acceptance is conditional, marked by four im (if) clauses that build from the initial condition (arm for battle) through the process (cross the Jordan) to the endpoint (land subdued before YHWH). The double 'before YHWH' (lifney YHWH) in v20-21 frames the military campaign as a divine-presence operation: they cross not merely with Israel but 'before YHWH,' in the march of the covenant presence.
21: ve'avar lachem kol chaluts et haYarden lifney YHWH: The crossing is 'before YHWH' — the ark of the covenant leads the march and the armed troops cross in relation to the covenant presence. The military and the liturgical are inseparable in Israel's conquest theology: the land is taken in YHWH's presence, under YHWH's authorization, and the armed force is technically the extension of YHWH's own army. To march 'before YHWH' is not a metaphor but a reference to the ark-procession of conquest.
22: veniqitem meYHWH umiyisrael: 'You will be free of obligation to YHWH and to Israel.' The completion of the military commitment releases them from both divine and communal obligation — the covenant duty has two dimensions: YHWH's claim (the land-conquest is divinely commanded) and Israel's claim (tribal solidarity requires shared sacrifice). Fulfilling both dimensions through armed vanguard service settles both accounts. The language anticipates the formal release Joshua gives the Transjordan tribes in Joshua 22:1-6.
23: uve'u chatatchem etckem: 'Know that your sin will find you out.' This single phrase has become a proverbial statement of moral accountability in English ('your sin will find you out'). The original context is Moses's conditional warning: if they break their military commitment, their sin (chet) will 'find' them — the verb matza (find) suggests the inevitability of consequence, as if sin were an active pursuer. The warning is not a threat but a statement of moral physics: covenant-breaking carries its own consequences regardless of whether anyone else enforces them.
24: Moses accepts the proposal: 'Go ahead — build towns for your children and pens for your flocks, and carry out what you have promised.' The phrase hayotzeh mipikem ta'asu (what has gone out of your mouth, do) invokes the principle of oath-fulfillment: spoken promises are binding. The permission to build domestically (cities for children, pens for flocks) is the practical implementation of the deal — Moses gives them what they asked for on the condition that they fulfill what they promised.
25: The formal acceptance: 'Your servants will do as my lord commands.' The language is the standard formula of covenant-acceptance in the ancient world — the subordinate party acknowledges the superior's authority and affirms compliance. 'Your servants' (avadeicha) and 'my lord' (adoni) establish the hierarchical relationship while marking the commitment as mutual and binding.
26: The specific arrangement: children (taph), wives (nashim), flocks (tzon), and all livestock stay in the cities of Gilead. The specificity of the list — every category of dependent — emphasizes that the men will leave behind everything that requires protection, trusting YHWH and Moses's arrangements to preserve their families while they fight. The risk of the commitment is real: their families are vulnerable; they are betting on YHWH's faithfulness to their covenant-keeping.
27: va'avadeicha ya'avru kol chaluts tzava lifney YHWH lamilchamah: 'All your servants, every armed man, will cross before YHWH to battle.' The repetition of 'before YHWH' (v21, v27) frames the military crossing as the central theological act. The armed service they offer is not secular soldiering but covenant warfare — marching in the manifest presence of YHWH as he fights for his people. This is the theological understanding of holy war (herem) warfare in the conquest tradition.
28: Moses records the agreement with Eleazar, Joshua, and the tribal heads — the full institutional leadership. The presence of Joshua in the covenant witnesses is significant: the Transjordan promise will be fulfilled under Joshua's command, not Moses's. Moses is recording a commitment that will be enforced by his successor. The formal witness list (priest + military leader + tribal heads) constitutes the maximum legal accountability in Israel's system.
29: The legal conditions stated formally: if the Gadites and Reubenites cross armed before YHWH and the land is subdued, then give them the Transjordan. The if-then structure mirrors the conditional covenant framework throughout Numbers: YHWH's promises are not unconditional grants but covenant commitments that require faithful response. The Transjordan inheritance is secured by their faithful military service, not by their having already claimed the land.
30: The sanction: if they do not fulfill the military commitment, 'they will receive their share in Canaan.' The sanction is not punishment per se but loss of the specific request — they would be forced to receive a Cisjordan inheritance like everyone else, rather than the Transjordan territory they desired. The sanction preserves their covenant status (they still get inheritance) while removing the specific accommodation they requested. This is proportionate consequence within the covenant framework.
31: ka'asher diber YHWH el avadeicha ken na'aseh: 'What the LORD has said, we will do.' The final acceptance is specifically attributed to YHWH's word through Moses — they are not merely agreeing with Moses's personal judgment but with YHWH's directive. The oath-formula includes 'we' — it is a communal commitment, binding on the entire Gadite and Reubenite population, not just the negotiating leaders. Josh 22 records the fulfillment: after the conquest, Joshua releases the Transjordan tribes to their inheritance.
32: The final statement of commitment: 'We will cross over armed before YHWH into Canaan, and our inherited possession will remain with us on this side of the Jordan.' The verse formally encapsulates the deal: the commitment (cross armed into Canaan) paired with the right retained (possession east of the Jordan). The written form of the agreement in v28-32 creates a legal record that functions as the covenant document for the Transjordan inheritance.
33: Moses assigns the Transjordan to Gad, Reuben, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. The inclusion of Manasseh here is the first mention of their Transjordan inheritance — Num 32 has focused on Gad and Reuben, but by the end of the chapter the Manassites have also acted to take Transjordan territory (v39-42). YHWH's grant includes 'the kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites and the kingdom of Og king of Bashan' — the two Amorite kingdoms whose defeat in Num 21 opened the Transjordan to Israelite settlement.
34: The rebuilding inventory begins with Gad: Dibon, Ataroth, and Aroer. The word yivnu (built/rebuilt) implies not new construction but reconstruction of existing cities. The conquering tribes are occupying and restoring pre-existing Amorite settlements, not building from scratch. This is the standard pattern of ancient near eastern conquest: the victor inherits the infrastructure of the conquered.
35: Additional Gadite rebuilds: Atroth-shophan, Jazer, and Jogbehah. The geographic spread of the cities across the Gilead region reflects the Gadites' claim to a substantial portion of the Transjordan territory. Jazer appears in v1 as one of the cities the petitioners identified as their desired territory — the rebuilding confirms that the original desire became the actual inheritance.
36: Beth-nimrah ('house of leopards' or 'house of clear water') and Beth-haran — 'all fortified cities' (arey mivtsar) plus sheepfolds (gidroth tzon). The pairing of fortified cities and sheepfolds is the dual requirement the tribes identified in their proposal (v16): they need both defensive strength for their families and functional pastoral infrastructure for their livestock. The final record confirms both needs were met.
37: Reubenite rebuilds: Heshbon, Elealeh, and Kiriathaim. Heshbon was Sihon's capital (Numbers 21:26-30) — the Reubenites claim the most prestigious Amorite city as their primary settlement. The choice of Sihon's former capital signals Reuben's claim to the leading position among the Transjordan tribes, consistent with Reuben's firstborn status among Jacob's sons (despite Reuben's historical loss of precedence, Genesis 49:3-4).
38: Nebo and Baal-meon — 'renaming them.' The command to rename cities whose names contain pagan divine names is the Israelite practice of covenant-marking: taking possession of idolatrous places and rededicating them by renaming. Baal-meon (house of Baal) is particularly obvious; Nebo (the Babylonian deity Nabu) is also a pagan name. The Reubenites rename them without preserving the pagan etymologies, though the text notes the original names for historical memory.
39: The sons of Machir son of Manasseh independently conquer Gilead and drive out the Amorites. The Manassites' military initiative is not explicitly authorized in the preceding negotiations — they take Gilead by conquest rather than by request. This independent military action by Machir echoes the broader pattern: the land is taken by those bold enough to go in and take it, consistent with the caleb-principle of 'following YHWH fully.'
40: Moses assigns Gilead formally to Machir — the military action (v39) is retrospectively authorized by Moses's official assignment (v40). The sequence (conquest then authorization) is the reverse of the Gad-Reuben pattern (request then conditional grant) but arrives at the same result: YHWH-backed Israelite settlement east of the Jordan. The variety of paths to settlement reflects the providential flexibility of the conquest: YHWH works through both formal negotiation and spontaneous military initiative.
41: Jair son of Manasseh takes the surrounding villages (chavvotam) and names them Havvoth-jair ('Jair's tent-villages'). The naming of places after their captor/settler is the ancient world's form of title deed — to name is to claim. The cluster of small settlements (chavvot) that Jair names for himself becomes a geographic marker in the Transjordan that endures into the period of the judges (Judges 10:3-4).
42: Nobah takes Kenath and its surrounding villages (venoteiha) and names the place after himself. The chapter ends with three individual acts of conquest (Machir, Jair, Nobah) — a pattern of personal initiative within the broader tribal assignment. Kenath is the northernmost of the Manassite Transjordan settlements, near the Hauran. The personal naming continues the chapter's theme of individuals claiming and naming their inheritance through personal courage.
